examples.title: Linear Search in C
examples.description: Basic iterative Linear Search implementation in C programming language
examples.explanation: The function iterates through the array from index 0 to n-1, comparing each element with the key. If a match is found, it immediately returns the index. If the loop completes without finding the key, it returns -1 to indicate absence.

examples.title: Linear Search in Python
examples.description: Linear Search implementation demonstrating the same algorithm in Python
examples.code:
def linear_search(arr, key):
    """
    Linear Search Algorithm
    Parameters:
        arr: List of elements
        key: Element to search for
    Returns:
        Index of key if found, -1 otherwise
    """
    for index in range(len(arr)):
        if arr[index] == key:
            return index
    return -1

# Example usage
arr = [10, 25, 30, 45, 50, 67]
key = 50

result = linear_search(arr, key)

if result != -1:
    print(f"Element found at index {result}")
else:
    print("Element not found in the array")
examples.explanation: Python version follows the same logic. The range(len(arr)) generates indices from 0 to length-1. The == operator checks equality, and the index is returned immediately upon finding a match.

examples.title: Linear Search in Java
examples.description: Linear Search implementation demonstrating the algorithm in Java with proper structure
examples.explanation: Java implementation uses a static method for the search algorithm. The array length is accessed using arr.length. The method returns an int (index or -1). Java's strong typing ensures type safety.

examples.title: Sentinel Linear Search Algorithm
examples.description: Optimized version of Linear Search that reduces one comparison per iteration
examples.explanation: The sentinel version eliminates the boundary check (i < n) in each iteration by placing the search key at the end of the array. The loop will always terminate either when the key is found or when it reaches the sentinel. This reduces comparisons from 2n to n+1 in the worst case.
